coredata.dc:title: Improved photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen generation through BiVO4 quantum-dots loaded on nano-structured SnO2 and modified with carbon quantum-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zemin
Jiang, Xiao
Mei, Junfeng
Li, Yunxia
Han, Weihua
Xie, Mingzheng
Wang, Fangcong
Xie, Erqing
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has been widely studied as a promising candidate for oxygen evolution in artificial photosynthesis. However, it was seldom used in hydrogen generation because of its low conduction band and high reaction overpotential. Here, we proposed an effective strategy to address these issues by distributing BiVO4 quantum dots onto nanostructured tin oxide (SnO2). The nanostructured SnO2 simultaneously served as the host substrate and a passivation layer. Carbon quantum dots were also used to improve the charge transfer and electrocatalytic properties at the BiVO4-SnO2 interface. As expected, the final composite yields a net photocurrent of 2.2mAcm−2 at 0.6Vvs. Ag/AgCl, and a hydrogen generation rate of 0.54μmol·h−1 without bias. The excellent performance was due to the enhanced electron energy, effective charge separation at the BiVO4-SnO2 interface, and the fast hole transfer from BiVO4 to carbon quantum dots and electrolyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen generation
Bismuth vanadate
Quantum effect
Carbon quantum dot
Charge transfer medium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Light absorption, charge separation and transfer dynamics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of the fabrication process, stainless steel mesh (a); NSO on the surface of stainless steel mesh (b); BVO-QDs decorated NSO (c) and C-QDs modified BVO-NSO (d).

Low and high magnification SEM images of NSO (a, b), BVO-NSO with different decorating cycles (c, d), TEM image of BVO-NSO (e) and HRTEM images of SNS (f) and BVO-QDs (g), the inset FFT pattern of NSO (f) and BVO (g).
XRD pattern of BVO-NSO (a), Raman spectra of BVO-NSO with or without C-QDs (b).

LSV curves of NSO and BVO-NSO (20 cycle) photocatalysts measured in 0.5M Na2SO4 with pH of 6.6 (a), Transient photocurrent of BVO-NSO photoanode at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl (b), LSV curves of BVO-NSO photocatalysts with or without C-QDs (c), and hydrogen generation rate of C-BVO-NSO photocatalyst (d).

UV–vis absorption spectra and plots of band gap of NSO, BVO-NSO-5 and BVO film (a, b), MS plots of NSO and BVO (c), schematic band structure of NSO, BVO film and BVO-QDs (d), SS-SPV signal of the BVO and BVO-NSO (e), Nyquist plots of BVO and BVO-NSO with or without C-QDs (f).

head.ce:title: Improved photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen generation through BiVO4 quantum-dots loaded on nano-structured SnO2 and modified with carbon quantum-dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Science, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Earth and Environmental Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Earth and Environmental Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China (W. Han).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has been widely studied as a promising candidate for oxygen evolution in artificial photosynthesis. However, it was seldom used in hydrogen generation because of its low conduction band and high reaction overpotential. Here, we proposed an effective strategy to address these issues by distributing BiVO4 quantum dots onto nanostructured tin oxide (SnO2). The nanostructured SnO2 simultaneously served as the host substrate and a passivation layer. Carbon quantum dots were also used to improve the charge transfer and electrocatalytic properties at the BiVO4-SnO2 interface. As expected, the final composite yields a net photocurrent of 2.2mAcm−2 at 0.6Vvs. Ag/AgCl, and a hydrogen generation rate of 0.54μmol·h−1 without bias. The excellent performance was due to the enhanced electron energy, effective charge separation at the BiVO4-SnO2 interface, and the fast hole transfer from BiVO4 to carbon quantum dots and electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution via water splitting, which can convert solar energy into storable chemical fuels, has been broadly recognized as a promising example of artificial photosynthesis [1]. However, the efficiency of water splitting is limited by the unfavorable properties of current photocatalysts, such as limited light absorption rate, low charge separation/transfer efficiency, etc [2–4]. Searching for new photocatalysts which are high efficient, inexpensive, and capable of harvesting visible light has been a long-term task of researchers in this filed for decades.

Generally, photocatalysts are metal oxide semiconductors, which are stable, low cost, and earth abundance. Among these photocatalysts, BiVO4 (BVO) has been widely investigated as a competitive candidate. BVO has a suitable band gap of (2.4–2.5eV) with maximum photocurrent density of 7.5mAcm−2 under AM 1.5G illumination [5–9]. However, for practical application, bulk BVO is limited because that its conduction band is close to the H+ reduction potential, which making it hard to produce hydrogen without external bias assistance [10]. Moreover, the trapping states at the FTO/BVO interfaces will increase the undesired charge recombination, and thus degrade the photoelectrocatalytic performance [11,12]. The poor oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics will lead to hole accumulation at the BVO/electrolyte interface, decrease the whole reaction rate and lead to an instable performance [13,14].

To address these issues, various strategies have been employed to improve the photoelectrocatalytic performance of BVO. Recent research shows that the conduction band of BVO can be evaluated when the size reduced to nanoscale due to the quantum confinement effect [10,15,16]. However, it is challenging to realize with BVO due to rapid crystalline growth [17]. Jiang et al. and Xie et al. have used tuber-like BVO in quantum size in diameter to produce hydrogen without bias. However, the hydrogen generation rate is extremely low due to the limited confinement effect and high reaction overpotential. BVO quantum dots (BVO-QDs) in three-dimensional quantum size, were believed to have a better photoelectrocatalytic performance. A critical feature in designing QDs photocatalyst is to balance the contradiction between loading amount and aggregation effect.

In this work, we used simple ionic layer absorption and reaction (SILAR) method to produce discretely distributed BVO-QDs on nanostructure SnO2 (NSO) host supporter (BVO-NSO) [18]. High-porous NSO can prevent the aggregation of BVO-QDs and serve as a passivation layer to the trapping states at the FTO/BVO interface to suppress charge recombination. In order to improve the OER kinetics, carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) were deposited on the BVO-NSO photocatalyst as the charge transfer medium and electrocatalyst [14,19]. As expected, the composite photocatalyst, C-QDs and BVO-QDs co-decorated on NSO (C-BVO-NSO), shows an excellent photoelectrocatalytic performance with high efficient water splitting hydrogen generation without bias.

The C-BVO-NSO composite photocatalyst was synthesized through the following procedures as diagramed in Fig. 1 . First, NSO was grown on stainless steel mesh piece by a hydrothermal method. Then, BVO-QDs were deposited onto the NSO surface through SILAR method. Finally, the prepared BVO-NSO was immersed into C-QDs solution and dried in vacuum oven to decorate C-QDs. The details of each procedure have provided as electronic supporting information (ESI).

The morphology change after each procedure has been tracked with scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) as shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a and b show the typical SEM images of the NSO at low and high magnification. It shows that the NSO is consists of vertically aligned SnO2 nanosheet (SNS) arrays with a thickness of 5nm. Such a two-dimensional (2D) structure can increase the photon-matter interaction via multiple reflection and scattering at the photocatalyst/electrolyte interface [20,21]. In addition, the porous SNS arrays with large surface area will serve as the host supporter for BVO-QDs to prevent aggregating. Fig. 2c and d are the SEM images of the BVO-NSO with different running cycles. They show that the surface of the SNS became rougher after the decoration of BVO-QDs. As the BVO amount increased, the SNS became thicker and the optical color of the samples changes from dark-gray to yellow-green (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 2e, the BVO-QDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of the SNS with an average diameter of 5nm. Such a perfect coating is due to the unique property of NSO and SILAR method. In SILAR process, the specific cations will strongly absorbed on the surface of nanostructure through chemical attraction force and physical Van-der Waals force, and then, water rinsing off the excess solution. Finally, the most strongly absorbed cations react with the less strongly absorbed anions and deposit on the surface of nanostructure [22]. The large surface area of the NSO will increase the loading of BVO-QDs and inhibit the aggregation of QDs at same time. Fig. 2f and g are the HRTEM images of the SNS and the BVO-QDs. Both the SNS and the BVO show perfect crystalline. The lattice spacing of 0.23±0.01nm (Fig. 2f) is indexed to the (111) plane of SnO2 (JCPDS No. 41-1445), and the ring patterns of Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) image (inset in Fig. 2f) indicates the SnO2 is polycrystalline. The lattice spacing of 0.32±0.01nm is consistent with the value of (111) plane of BVO (Fig. 2g). Comparing the FFT image of BVO-NSO with that of NSO, the additional spots are attributed to the BVO-QDs, which implies the BVO-QDs is well crystalline and dispersive.

We studied the diameter distribution of the C-QDs with TEM as shown in Fig. S2a. The diameter of the C-QDs ranges from 2.5 to 4.0nm with an average value of 3.5nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed to confirm the quantum confinement effect of the C-QDs. Fig. S2b shows a strong emission peak at 513nm with the excitation wavelength of 340nm, which may be attribute to the size effect and the existence of functional groups at the surface [23]. Fig. S3 are the SEM and TEM images of the produced C-BVO-NSO. After modifying with C-QDs, the SEM image shows no obviously change, while in the TEM image, both the BVO-QDs and C-QDs can been found.The crystal structure of the nanocomposites has been investigated by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the diffraction peaks of the NSO are well fitted with that of SnO2 (JCPDS No. 41-1445) and stainless steel (JCPDS No. 31-0619). After being decorated with BVO-QDs, a wide peak around 28.8° appears, which can be attributed to the (
1
‾
3
0
), (
1
‾
2
1
) and (121) peaks of the BVO (JCPDS No. 14-0688). The weak peak intensity of the BVO may due to the low mass fraction in the BVO-NSO composite. The XRD studies confirms the bi-phasic nature of the composition, SnO2 and BVO. As shown in Fig. S4, the XRD patterns of C-BVO-NSO shows no change with that of BVO-NSO. Therefore, the Raman spectroscopy was applied to detect the existence of the C-QDs (Fig. 3b). The peaks in the spectrum of BVO-NSO are all consistent with SnO2, BVO and Fe2O3 (came from the oxide of stainless steel during high-temperature annealing process) [24,25]. The peak at 825cm−1 is the characteristic peak of BVO [26]. After modifying C-QDs, a new peak appears at 1605cm−1, which is consistent with the G band of carbon material. In addition, the peak at 1331cm−1 becomes stronger compared with that of BVO-NSO, which can be separated into two peaks at 1325cm−1 and 1352cm−1. The peak at 1352cm−1 corresponds to the D band of carbon material. The appearance and the relative high-intensity rate of D/G bands indicates the existence of the defects on C-QDs [23,27].

The photoelectrocatalytic performance of the produced composite was evaluated by LSV measurements. Fig. 4 a demonstrates the photocurrent density as a function of applied bias voltage under simulated solar light irradiation (100mWcm−2). The NSO photocatalyst yields a low-level net photocurrent (the difference of the photocurrent and dark-current) about 0.2mAcm−2 at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl. The pure BVO photocatalyst is about 0.9mAcm−2 at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl, due to its narrow band gap (Fig. S5a). However, the net photocurrent of BVO-NSO composite increased to 0.95mAcm−2 at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl. More interesting, the fill factor of the BVO-NSO composite is significantly large than that of BVO photocatalyst. Such a significant enhancement may ascribe to the unique 3D structure, suitable band alignment between NSO and BVO, and the NSO passivation layer effect as well [11,12,28]. The photoelectrocatalytic performance of the produced photocatalyst is strongly depended on the amount of the BVO-QDs. To reveal the amount effect of the BVO-QDs, a set of LSV were performed on the samples with various BVO-QDs amount controlled by decorating cycles (Fig. S3b). When increase the BVO-QDs amount, the photocurrent will increase at low amount, and stop increasing after reaching a saturation state. Applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) has been calculated to find the optimal amount of the BVO-QDs and the results show that the optimized running cycle is 20 (0.49mg) as shown in Fig. S6a. The decreased ABPE when further increasing the running cycles may due to the aggregation of the BVO-QDs which has a negative effect on the photoelectrocatalytic performance [29]. From now on, all the following tests based on BVO-NSO is 20 cycle. Transient photocurrent response was examined by chopped light chronoamperometry at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl. (Fig. 4b). The positive transient current upon turning the light on represents the accumulation of holes at the BVO-NSO/electrolyte interface without injection to the electrolyte. While, the negative transients current upon turning the light off represents the back recombination of electrons from the conduction band with the accumulated holes [25,30]. In order to accelerate the holes injection, C-QDs were used as the charge transfer medium and electrocatalyst to modify the photocatalyst (Fig. 4c) [15,31]. The net photocurrent of C-BVO-NSO increased significantly to 2.2mAcm−2 at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl, which is about twice than that of BVO-NSO.

Incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) has also been measured as a function of wavelength from 330nm to 650nm at 0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. S6b). The IPCE of BVO-NSO is as high as 9% at 370nm and exhibits a significant enhancement with the wavelength less than 520nm. After modifying with C-QDs, the IPCE increased to 14%. This result is consist with the change of photocurrent (Fig. 4b).

The practical hydrogen generation rate of the photocatalyst was measured by gas chromatography (Fig. 4d). The electrolyte is DI water (100ml) with methanol (10ml) as hole scavenge. As previous reported, the bulk BVO cannot generate hydrogen from water splitting without bias due to the close conduction band of bulk BVO to the H2O reduction potential [10,32]. However, when decreasing the BVO size into QDs, the C-BVO-NSO photocatalyst can yield a hydrogen generation rate about 0.54μmol·h−1 without bias, which is ranked among the top reported value (Table S1). Such an excellent photoelectrocatalytic performance of C-BVO-NSO photocatalyst is majorly determined by the excellent light absorption, charge separation and transfer [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light absorption, charge separation and transfer dynamics
ce:section.ce:para: Their optical properties were comparably studied with UV–vis absorption spectra. The NSO can only absorb the photons which wavelength is less than 350nm. After sensitizing with BVO-QDs (5 cycle), the maximum wavelength shows an obviously red shift to 480nm (Fig. 5 a). In order to confirm the quantum confinement effect, the absorption spectrum of BVO-NSO-5 has been compared with that of BVO film produced by electrodeposition. BVO film shows a strong light absorption with the wavelength less than 500nm, which is large than that of BVO-QDs. The band gap has also been calculated with the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. 5b). The band gap of the NSO is about 3.7eV. After decorating with BVO, the band gap decreased to 3.0eV. However, the band gap of BVO film is 2.5eV which is small than that of BVO-QDs. This result indicates the excellent quantum confinement effect.

Steady-state surface photovoltage (SS-SPV) technology has used to investigate their photoelectrical properties, such as the charge separation and transmission process at the interface of photocatalyst, and the role of the NSO in the photoelectrocatalytic reaction. For pure BVO, plenty of photogenerated carriers will generate when the photon energy of the monochromatic light is great than its band gap. The SS-SPV signal generated as the carriers separated in the space [36]. The positive SPV response implies that the photogenerated holes move to the front side surface of the sample. Fig. 5e shows the SS-SPV spectra of pure BVO and BVO-NSO. Two peaks can be found in the spectra. The weak peak at the range of 480–520nm in SPV spectra can be attribute to the defects of material. This phenomenon also can be find in IPCE spectra (Fig. S6b). The pure BVO shows only a weak positive signal in about 440nm region. This mainly caused by the low separation rate of the photogenerated charges at the FTO/BVO interface. However, when NSO and BVO combined to BVO-NSO junctions, the SS-SPV signal increased significantly. This phenomenon indicates that the heterojunction formed at the interface of BVO-NSO can accelerate the accumulation of photogenerated holes at the light illuminated side. This result is consistent with the analysis in Fig. 5d. Under the action of the interfacial electric field, the photogenerated electrons in BVO can transfer to NSO and the same amount of holes accumulated at the surface of BVO. Therefore, NSO has the function of collecting photogenerated electrons, which will promote the electron-hole separation. Interestingly, after being modified with C-QDs, the SS-SPV signal increased obviously, which indicates that the C-QDs has the function to collect holes on the BVO surface, which can facilitate more holes to inject into the electrolyte and participate in water splitting reaction.

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) measurement was applied to investigate the change of charge transfer kinetics with/without C-QDs layer during water splitting (Fig. 5f). The corresponding Nyquist plots were fitted with RC (resistance and capacitance) circuit model (Fig. S7). Rs is the series resistance of the electrochemical system. The first RC circuit (R1, CEP1) regards as the charge transfer resistance and the capacitance of the semiconductor layer. Meanwhile, due to the most difficult reaction-taking place at the interface of semiconductor/electrolyte, the biggest resistance (R2) generally observed here. Comparing the plots of BVO-NSO with or without light illumination, the R1 and R2 show a significant decrease, while Rs almost keeps the same. This is due to the increased conductivity of the semiconductor caused by photoexcitation. After modifying with C-QDs, the Rs decreased due to the excellent electron conductivity of carbon-based materials. In addition, R2 also shows a significant decrease under light illumination, indicating C-QDs with abundant defects can work as the charge transfer medium to accelerate the hole injection from semiconductor to electrolyte. During photoexcitation process, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs will be separated by the build-in electric field and transfer to NSO and BVO, respectively, and then the holes on BVO may easily transfer to C-QDs through the π-π interaction. At last and the most important, the large amount hydrophilic oxide groups may work as the bridge to contact C-QDs and the electrolyte, which will accelerate the hole injection from C-QDs to electrolyte and take part in water oxidation reaction.

We have solve the problem of hardly water splitting hydrogen generation without external bias of BVO based photocatalyst by designing a C-DQs modified BVO-NSO photocatalyst. In this structure, BVO-QDs serve as the primary light absorber to support electrons with enough energy. NSO works as the substrate for BVO-QDs deposition to overcome the problem of aggregation. NSO layer also serves as the passivation layer for the trapping states at FTO/BVO interface to suppress charge recombination. C-QDs with enriched defects simultaneously serve as the charge transfer medium and electrocatalyst to improving the OER kinetics. Due to the effective quantum confinement effect, the conduction band of BVO-QDs is elevated to −0.5V vs. RHE, which is negative than that of water reduction (0V vs. RHE). According to the analysis of MS and SS-SPV, a type II heterojunction has been formed at the NSO/BVO interface with an interface electric field from NSO to BVO, which could enhance the charge separation. EIS analysis indicates that the holes on BVO can easily transfer to C-QDs. We attribute this effect to the π-π interaction. Plenty of hydrophilic oxide groups on C-QDs may work as the electron bridge, which can accelerate the hole injection from C-QDs to the electrolyte and take part in water oxidation reaction. This work is applicable to other metal oxide semiconductor nanomaterial with shortcomings as BVO for photoelectrocatalytic water splitting hydrogen generation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. U1531114, 51572118 and 61404066) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (China, Grant No. lzujbky-2017-180).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.08.102.
